questions.questionText: Proton pump inhibitors (e.g., omeprazole) act by:
questions.optionB: Irreversibly inhibiting H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on parietal cell → most potent acid suppression
questions.explanation: PPIs: irreversibly inhibit H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on apical membrane of parietal cells → block final step of acid secretion (regardless of stimulus). Most effective acid suppressants. Prodrugs: activated in acidic canaliculus of parietal cell. Uses: GERD, PUD, ZES, H. pylori eradication (triple therapy), stress ulcer prophylaxis. Side effects (long-term): hypomagnesemia, osteoporosis/fractures, C. difficile infection, vitamin B12 deficiency, fundic gland polyps. Reference: KD Tripathi — PPIs.

questions.optionB: Forming a protective barrier by binding to positively charged proteins in the ulcer base (polymerizes at pH <4)
questions.explanation: Sucralfate: aluminum hydroxide + sulfated sucrose. At acidic pH (<4): polymerizes → viscous paste → adheres to ulcer crater (binds positively charged proteins exposed in necrotic tissue) → physical protective barrier against acid, pepsin, bile. Also stimulates PGE2 and mucus secretion. Requires acidic environment (give on empty stomach, avoid with PPIs/antacids). Minimal systemic absorption. Uses: duodenal ulcer, stress ulcer prophylaxis. Side effect: constipation (aluminum). Reference: KD Tripathi — Sucralfate.

questions.questionText: Ondansetron is the preferred antiemetic for chemotherapy-induced vomiting because it blocks:
questions.optionB: 5-HT3 serotonin receptors (in CTZ and vagal afferents)
questions.explanation: Ondansetron: 5-HT3 receptor antagonist → blocks serotonin in CTZ (chemoreceptor trigger zone) and peripheral vagal afferents in GI tract. Most effective for chemotherapy-induced (especially cisplatin) and radiation-induced nausea/vomiting, PONV. Enhanced by dexamethasone. For highly emetogenic chemo: add NK1 antagonist (aprepitant). Other antiemetics: metoclopramide (D2 + 5-HT4), domperidone (D2, doesn't cross BBB), scopolamine (muscarinic — motion sickness). Reference: KD Tripathi — Ondansetron.

questions.questionText: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal that acts by:
questions.optionB: Activating μ-opioid receptors in the gut wall → decreases peristalsis and increases anal sphincter tone (does NOT cross BBB)
questions.explanation: Loperamide: synthetic opioid, acts on μ-opioid receptors in myenteric plexus of gut wall → decreases peristalsis, increases segmentation contractions, increases transit time, increases water/electrolyte absorption, increases anal sphincter tone. Does NOT cross BBB (P-glycoprotein substrate) → no CNS effects/addiction at therapeutic doses. OTC antidiarrheal. Contraindicated in: bloody diarrhea, toxic megacolon, C. difficile colitis, dysentery. Diphenoxylate: similar but some CNS effects (combined with atropine). Reference: KD Tripathi — Loperamide.

questions.questionText: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic drug that acts by:
questions.optionB: D2 receptor antagonism (central antiemetic + peripheral prokinetic) and 5-HT4 agonism (enhances GI motility)
questions.explanation: Metoclopramide: (1) D2 antagonist in CTZ → antiemetic, (2) D2 antagonist in GI → prokinetic (removes dopamine inhibition of ACh release), (3) 5-HT4 agonist in GI → enhances ACh release → coordinated gastric contractions → accelerates gastric emptying. Uses: gastroparesis (diabetic), GERD, post-operative nausea, chemotherapy nausea. Side effects: EPS (dystonia, tardive dyskinesia — crosses BBB), hyperprolactinemia (galactorrhea, gynecomastia). Domperidone: D2 blocker, does NOT cross BBB → fewer EPS. Reference: KD Tripathi — Metoclopramide.
